div1.entryFree: Hose (unchanged in the plur.) breeches, trowsers: to garter his h. Gent. II, 1, 83. to put on your h. Gent. II, 1, 83* a round h. now's not worth a pin, unless you have a codpiece to stick pins on, II, 7, 55. rhymes are guards on wanton Cupid's h. LLL IV, 3, 58. his round h. Merch. I, 2, 80. his youthful h. a world too wide for his shrunk shank, As II, 7, 160. your h. should be ungartered, III, 2, 397 (after the manner of lovers; cf. Gent. II, 1, 83). dost make h. of thy sleeves? All's II, 3, 266. thrust through the h. H4A II, 4, 185. down fell their h. H4A II, 4, 185 French h. H5 III, 7, 57. Mcb. II, 3, 16. Doublet and h. constituting the principal parts of a male dress: in your doublet and h. Wiv. III, 1, 47 (i.e. without a cloak); cf. Ado V, 1, 203 and H6B IV, 7, 56. Wiv. III, 3, 35. As II, 4, 7. III, 2, 206. III, 2, 206 IV, 1, 206. Shr. V, 1, 69. Cymb. III, 4, 172.
